skill.name: daem0nmcp-protocol
skill.description: Guides agents to use Daem0nMCP memory tools for session briefings, context checks, decision capture, outcome tracking, and memory graph review.
skill.summary: Use Daem0nMCP memory tools to check context, record decisions, and track outcomes across coding sessions.
skill.author: DasBluEyedDevil

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance findings are false positives from markdown examples and descriptive terms, not executable code. The real risk is medium because the skill mandates Daem0nMCP memory calls that can persist project decisions, file paths, outcomes, specs, and indexed code context. No malicious intent or prompt-injection attempt was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Mandatory MCP Memory Calls Can Persist Project Context
medium_findings.description: The protocol requires calls that record decisions, rationales, file paths, outcomes, and linked memories. This is legitimate for a memory workflow, but it can retain sensitive project context if users do not understand the Daem0nMCP storage boundary.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented tool calls explicitly persist content, rationale, file paths, outcomes, and relationships. The risk depends on Daem0nMCP deployment and retention settings, so this is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Required Pre-Edit Tool Calls Reduce User Consent
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to call get_briefing before asking the user what they want and to perform context checks before any file change. Automatic use may reveal task descriptions or project context to the MCP server before explicit per-task consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions are explicit and mandatory, including a directive not to ask the user first. The finding concerns consent and data flow, not proven malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Code Indexing and Spec Import May Expose Repository Content
medium_findings.description: The skill documents automatic OpenSpec import and code intelligence tools for project indexing, semantic code search, and impact analysis. These capabilities may send repository structure, specs, and code entities into persistent MCP memory or indexes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file names concrete indexing and import workflows that read repository artifacts. No exfiltration endpoint is shown, but the MCP server storage boundary must be trusted.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Findings Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The detected Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns are not executable code. They appear inside markdown fences, tool names, relationship labels, or descriptive text such as cryptographic proof and SHA256 content hashes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is markdown guidance with fenced examples and no scripts or executable Ruby. The flagged text does not invoke shell execution or weak cryptographic operations.

content.value_statement: AI assistants often lose project decisions and repeat failed approaches across sessions. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to check Daem0nMCP memory, record decisions, and track outcomes.
actual_capabilities: Detect whether Daem0nMCP memory tools are available before applying the workflow.
Require a session briefing to surface past decisions, warnings, patterns, and recent changes.
Guide context checks and file-specific recall before code edits.
Record decisions, rationale, tags, file paths, and implementation outcomes.
Link and trace related memories through a knowledge graph.
Describe code indexing, semantic code search, and impact analysis workflows.
limitations: It only works when Daem0nMCP MCP tools are installed and available.
It does not implement the MCP server or memory storage itself.
It can add workflow overhead for small or low-risk tasks.
Memory privacy depends on the Daem0nMCP server configuration and retention policy.

use_cases.title: Preserve architectural decisions
use_cases.description: Record decisions, rationales, and outcomes so future sessions can reuse project history.

use_cases.title: Avoid repeated failures
use_cases.description: Surface warnings and failed approaches before new implementation work begins.

use_cases.title: Trace decision relationships
use_cases.description: Use memory links and graphs to understand how related technical choices evolved.

prompt_templates.title: Start With Memory
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: start a new coding session.
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether Daem0nMCP tools are available. If they are available, get the session briefing before starting work.

prompt_templates.title: Check Before Editing
prompt_templates.prompt: Before editing files, check Daem0nMCP context for the planned change and recall file-specific memory for the affected files.

prompt_templates.title: Record a Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Record this technical decision in Daem0nMCP with the rationale, related file path, useful tags, and any constraints that affected the choice.

prompt_templates.title: Close the Loop
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: finish implementation and learning capture.
prompt_templates.prompt: After testing the implementation, record whether the decision worked, summarize the outcome, and link related memories that influenced the result.

output_examples.input: Start work on the authentication feature with Daem0nMCP available.
output: The assistant loads the project briefing first.
It reports relevant past authentication decisions and warnings.
It uses the returned context to shape the next implementation step.

output_examples.input: Prepare to edit a service file that may affect payment logic.
output: The assistant checks context for the planned change.
It recalls memory tied to the target file.
It highlights any warning before editing begins.

output_examples.input: Finish a refactor that changed the caching approach.
output: The assistant records the implementation outcome.
It notes whether the approach worked after testing.
It links the outcome to the original caching decision.

best_practices: Confirm Daem0nMCP storage and retention settings before using the skill with sensitive repositories.
Summarize decisions clearly so future sessions can understand both the choice and the rationale.
Record failed approaches with the same care as successful outcomes.
anti_patterns: Using the skill with private code before confirming where Daem0nMCP stores memory.
Recording vague decisions without rationale, affected files, or useful tags.
Skipping outcome tracking after implementation because the work seemed complete.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides an assistant to use Daem0nMCP memory tools at session start, before edits, after decisions, and after implementation.

faq.question: Does it work without Daem0nMCP?
faq.answer: No. If Daem0nMCP tools are not available, the skill says the protocol does not apply.

faq.question: Does the skill execute shell commands?
faq.answer: No. The file contains markdown instructions and MCP tool-call examples, not executable shell or Ruby code.

faq.question: What data may be stored?
faq.answer: The workflow may store decisions, rationales, file paths, outcomes, tags, linked memories, specs, and indexed code context.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The workflow is legitimate, but it can persist sensitive repository context through an external MCP memory system.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: Use it when your project intentionally relies on Daem0nMCP for persistent memory and the storage boundary is trusted.
